title: A tragedy prompted this American to build a new life in Prague. 25 years later, he’s still there
full_text:
He’s been living in Prague for well over two decades, but Ryan Goodwin, originally from Colorado, says the awe still hits him each time he crosses the city’s landmark Charles Bridge and looks up at the medieval castle sprawling across the skyline.

“It never ceases to amaze me,” Goodwin tells CNN Travel. He says local writer Franz Kafka best sums up the Czech capital’s sustained allure in the quote: “Prague never lets you go … This dear little mother has sharp claws.”

“I think that’s so true,” adds Goodwin. “This culture. The city. It just grabs you … It’s been a really fun life. I’m glad that I chose this place.”

The Czech Republic wasn’t on Goodwin’s radar while growing up in Colorado — he’d barely left the US before his first trip there — but his perspective on life changed completely in 1999, when a tragedy occurred at his former school, Columbine High School.

From that moment on, Goodwin, who was in college elsewhere at the time of the deadly school attack ranked as one of the worst mass shootings in US history, says he was fiercely determined to leave.

“I wanted to go away, and that was sort of like the moment that did it,” he says. “It was like, ‘I don’t want to be in a place where that is a possibility. I don’t want to worry about my kids going to school and not coming home.’”

A few months later, at the age of 21, Goodwin traveled to Prague for a four-month study abroad program along with a friend.

While he hadn’t planned to stay in the European country beyond that length of time, he went on to meet his now-wife Marketa, who is Czech, at a student pub shortly before he was due to return home in December 1999.

Over 25 years later, Goodwin is now a permanent resident of the Czech Republic and has two children who were born there.

While he initially had no intention of staying long in Prague, he says something “clicked” after he connected with Marketa. When he left soon after, he knew he had to find a way back to the “City of a Hundred Spires.”

In September 2000, less than a year after heading home to the United States to finish his business administration degree, Goodwin was back in Prague. He moved into the dormitory he’d previously stayed in and began teaching at a local university.

The Czech Republic was “still kind of a wild place” at the time, he says, with many international residents coming and going.

“It was just the right amount of excitement without feeling like I was getting in over my head,” he adds.

Back then, Goodwin considered himself a revolutionary and was convinced he could “really make a difference and a change” in a country that had only recently emerged from the dissolution of the former Czechoslovakia and from communist rule just over a decade earlier.

“But I learned early on, one thing about Czech culture is that they like the way things are,” he says. “They don’t like to rock the boat.”

Despite having to adjust his expectations, Goodwin found that life in Prague suited him immensely — he loved the slower pace and felt far removed from the “rat race” he’d witnessed back home.

“In my view, Czechs don’t have that endless drive for bigger and better that we have in the US,” he says. “The feeling of ‘less is more’ is acceptable.”

As time went on, Goodwin felt less connected to the States and more at home in the Czech Republic.

“I remember my mother asking me for the first few years, ‘Are you done with this?’” he says. “And I was like, ‘No.’”

While he’d envisioned himself returning home at some stage, Goodwin says that another monumental event in the US, the terror attacks of September 11, 2001, cemented his decision to stay in the Czech Republic for good.

“All these things come to your head,” he said. “And then I just thought, ‘This is safe here.’ Prague has always been such a safe place … Why go anywhere else?”

As his relationship with Marketa became more serious, it became increasingly apparent to him that he was where he was meant to be, and he felt fully committed to building a life in Prague.

“There was never any consideration after that,” he says. “It was, ‘How do we live here now?’ instead of, ‘Do we live here now?”

In 2005, the couple tied the knot and went on to have a son and a daughter, who hold dual citizenship.

Goodwin says that he’s thrilled to be raising his kids in a country that’s so accepting.

“As a culture, Czechs are such amazing people,” he says. “They’re so welcoming here.”

While he often feels caught between two identities as an American with a dual-nationality family, he’s adjusted relatively well over the years.

However, Goodwin admits he initially struggled with what he describes as “passive-aggressive” tendencies when it comes to communication in the country.

“They won’t just come out and confront you about something,” he says. “So, there’s this kind of live and let live attitude, even if people are upset with each other.”

He recalls how he often assumed that shop workers in the Czech Republic were being deliberately rude but came to realize that the customer service approach was far more hands off than what he was used to.

In fact, Goodwin jokingly recalls how his wife ran out of a store during one of their trips to the US together after being approached by staff several times and feeling “attacked at all sides.”

Although he speaks Czech, Goodwin describes it as a “hard language.” He says he feels he’ll never be fluent and sometimes frustrates his wife by relying on her to communicate for him in situations where there’s little room for misunderstandings. But he keeps trying.

“This is definitely a culture where if you try with the language, they really appreciate it,” he says.

As for the cost of living, Goodwin says he found the Czech capital to be very affordable up until around a decade ago.

While he considered himself to be financially stable, he realized that he needed to make some changes to make things more comfortable for his family as prices increased, particularly as his children were getting older and wanted more space.

After watching an episode of the UK TV show “Grand Designs,” which follows people as they build their dream house, Goodwin and his wife, who were living in a home overlooking the Vltava River at the time, decided to “get creative” and build their own floating house.

Although they didn’t have enough for a down payment at first, they were later able to use inheritance money that Goodwin had received from his parents after they both passed away a few years earlier.

“My parents would love this house so much,” he says of their three-bedroom home, moored in a marina next to the central Prague neighborhood of Holešovice, just a short walk from the pre-school the couple opened in 2013. “They were always so happy and supportive of us.”

While he used to return to the US regularly, Goodwin concedes that his interest going back has declined due to the current political and social climate and the death of his parents.

“I don’t even want to put myself into it,” he says, raising concerns about how his wife’s non-American status would be treated at a time when foreign nationals are coming under greater scrutiny in the US.

“I don’t want to put my wife at risk … You start reading the horror stories, and you’re just like, ‘Why?’”

Although he misses Colorado and his friends and family, Goodwin says their absence isn’t enough to pull him back at the moment.

However, he still feels hugely connected to his hometown and remains close with the friends he went to school with, citing the community slogan, “We are Columbine.”

“It’s part of everybody,” he says, before stressing that there was a definite “shift in the culture” locally after the tragedy. “I can only hope that this many years later, that it’s still there.”

For Goodwin, the freedom from “that type of fear” remains one of the biggest draws of life in the Czech Republic.

“We all have our problems, but that’s not one I feel that we have here,” he says, recalling a recent conversation with the son of a friend in the United States, who was describing going through active shooter drills at his school. “Don’t we all just want to live without that worry?”

He adds: “I love the United States, but I love that it’s there. I love that it’s waiting when I want to go home. But this is home.”

Goodwin often drives past the dormitory he lived in when he first arrived in the Czech Republic all those years ago and can’t help but marvel at how far he’s come.

“It’s like looking back at myself,” he says. “Like saying, ‘Hey guy. It’s going to be good. Everything’s gonna be okay.’

“Who would have thought, when I was looking out a window on that street, that in 25 or 26 years, I was going to be driving by in my car, going to my CrossFit class.

“Life is such a wild adventure. I’m so happy that it brought me here.”
